topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone, startling me mid-scroll. It was a picture. A candid shot of me from the school play, taken from a slightly awkward angle, and it was tagged with Mr. Harrison's account. My breath hitched. I stared at the screen, my thumb hovering over the 'unfollow' button, but my fingers felt strangely heavy. He was posting photos of students. Not just class announcements, but actual, personal moments. A slow, uneasy feeling settled in my stomach, like a cold stone dropped into warm water. I quickly minimized the app, pretending to be intensely interested in a history lecture happening across the room. Every time I glanced toward the classroom door, I felt a prickle of awareness, a sense that the boundaries of our relationship had just shifted, inexplicably and unsettlingly.

[story 2]

I was browsing through my feed, completely absorbed in an unrelated thread about astrophysics, when I saw it. A photo of me, standing by the library window, looking thoughtful. The caption, written in his distinctive, precise handwriting, was simply: "Reflecting on the complexities of narrative structure." My jaw tightened. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. Why was he documenting these moments? It wasn't creepy, exactly—it was just… unexpected. A strange tension built behind my eyes. I tried to rationalize it: maybe it was a shared class project, maybe it was a misunderstanding. But the way my heart was beating felt too fast, a frantic little drum against my ribs. I couldn't look away from the screen, unable to pull my gaze back to the textbook in front of me.

[story 3]

It happened during the lunch break. I was eating my sandwich alone, trying to ignore the usual lunchtime chaos, when my phone buzzed. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. It contained a selfie he’d clearly taken of me earlier that week, taken perhaps in the hallway, and the message simply read, "Hope you're having a productive afternoon." I dropped my plastic fork onto the tray with a clatter. My hands started to shake slightly. I looked around the cafeteria, suddenly hyper-aware of every passing student, every whispered conversation. A flush crept up my neck, spreading across my cheeks. I felt an overwhelming need to check his profile again, to see if there was anything else there, anything to explain this bizarre intrusion into my private space. The silence in my own head was deafening.
